findings.title: Confirmations-off, client-direct signup opens AI cost-amplification via account farming
findings.detail: Signup is a direct browser/app -> Supabase GoTrue call (src/app/components/onboarding/steps/signup.tsx) with email confirmation OFF, so signUp() returns a usable session synchronously. It never passes through Suppr's rateLimit() helper — there is no server-side signup proxy (rate-limits.md sec.4). All expensive AI endpoints (photo-log vision, voice-log, coach LLM, recipe-import video transcription via Supadata/Fal/Anthropic/OpenAI) are metered per-userId (rate-limits.md sec.2). Because the meters key on userId, a scripted attacker who farms fresh accounts resets every free-tier quota per account and drives real AI/vendor spend with no aggregate ceiling. The only floor is GoTrue's built-in per-IP signup caps, which this repo neither configures nor verifies (rate-limits.md sec.4 explicitly flags this as an unrecorded gap). With the viral push live and genuinely adversarial users this week, this is the most material unmetered-cost vector on the surface.
findings.recommendation: Do two things before scaling the push: (1) verify and tighten Supabase GoTrue signup/OTP rate limits in the dashboard, and turn email confirmation ON (or add a server-side signup proxy behind rateLimit() with getTrustedClientIp keying) so account creation isn't free-and-instant; (2) add an aggregate cost ceiling / anomaly alert on the AI vendor spend (Anthropic/OpenAI/Fal/Supadata) so account-farming cannot silently drain budget overnight. Track as a launch-urgent item owned by Grace (GoTrue config lives in the dashboard, not the repo).

findings.title: recipe_ingredients & recipe_steps are world-readable (USING true) — leaks unpublished/draft recipe content cross-user and to anon
findings.detail: recipes SELECT is correctly gated (recipes_select_published_own_or_saved: published OR own OR saved). But the child tables are not: recipe_ingredients_select_public and recipe_steps_select_public both use qual USING (true) for role {public} (policies.md), and grants.md confirms anon holds SELECT on both. A holder of the anon key — authenticated or fully anonymous — can bulk-read every row of recipe_ingredients and recipe_steps via a single unfiltered PostgREST query, including the full ingredient lists and cooking steps of other users' UNPUBLISHED/draft recipes. The parent recipe's title stays hidden but recipe_id links the content back. For a product whose core asset and viral hook is recipe content, this is a clean cross-user content exposure of private drafts. Classic broken object-level authorization on child tables.
findings.recommendation: Replace the USING (true) SELECT policies on recipe_ingredients and recipe_steps with an EXISTS check that mirrors the parent recipes SELECT policy (parent published = true OR parent author_id = auth.uid() OR parent saved by the caller). This preserves public sharing of PUBLISHED recipes (the viral path) while closing draft leakage. Ship via migration + supabase db push --linked.

findings.title: Direct client INSERT into barcode_mappings enables shared-table nutrition poisoning, bypassing the rate-limited API route
findings.detail: barcode_mappings is a shared, world-readable community table (barcode_mappings_select_public USING true) and authenticated holds a direct INSERT grant plus a permissive policy (barcode_mappings_write_own, with_check auth.uid() = created_by). The intended write path /api/barcode-mapping is service-role, per-user rate-limited (30/60s), origin-checked and attribution-tagged (auth-surface.md) — but RLS also lets any authenticated user POST straight to /rest/v1/barcode_mappings, unmetered and unvalidated, creating arbitrary barcode->food mappings that are immediately served to every user who scans that barcode. Unlike user_foods (which is gated by verification_status + flag/vote moderation + an admin-only status-transition guard trigger), barcode_mappings has no moderation between insert and public read. Project memory marks nutrition trust as CRITICAL, so a bulk poisoning run directly undermines the trust surface.
findings.recommendation: Force all barcode_mappings writes through the validated, rate-limited API route: REVOKE INSERT/UPDATE on barcode_mappings from anon and authenticated and drop barcode_mappings_write_own / barcode_mappings_update_own (the service-role route bypasses RLS and keeps working). If direct client writes are required, add server-mediated validation + the same per-user rate limit at the DB layer. Confirm a UNIQUE constraint on barcode so a single canonical mapping wins.

findings.title: recipe_collections migration merged to main but never applied to prod — feature is dead and throws 'relation does not exist' for all users
findings.detail: supabase/migrations/20260703140000_eng1126_recipe_collections.sql (merged in afe735a9, the ENG-1126 collections PR shipped in web + mobile clients) creates recipe_collections, recipe_collection_items, the auth_owns_collection() helper and 7 RLS policies. None of it exists live: no rows in information_schema.tables for either table, neither appears in policies.md/rls-status.md/grants.md, and supabase_migrations.schema_migrations has no row for version 20260703140000 (rls-drift.md Finding #1, confirmed by read-only execute_sql). db push --linked was silently never run for this file. Any client path added in that PR that reads/writes collections fails at the database for every production user right now. This is a functional regression, not a security exposure, but it is a live release blocker — and it is exactly the migration-drift failure mode CLAUDE.md warns about.
findings.recommendation: Stage 20260703140000_eng1126_recipe_collections.sql and have Grace run supabase db push --linked (never via MCP apply_migration, per the non-negotiable). It is the newest file in the tree and nothing more recent depends on it, so the push is low-risk. Add a CI/release check that fails when a merged migration file has no corresponding schema_migrations row, so a merged-but-unpushed migration can't recur silently.

findings.title: EXPO_PUBLIC_E2E_PASSWORD would bake a plaintext login credential into the shipped mobile bundle if ever set at build
findings.detail: apps/mobile/context/auth.tsx has an E2E seam: when EXPO_PUBLIC_E2E_AUTH_ENABLED === 'true' and EXPO_PUBLIC_E2E_EMAIL + EXPO_PUBLIC_E2E_PASSWORD are set, the app auto-signs-in with those plaintext credentials, bypassing the login UI. EXPO_PUBLIC_* vars are inlined by Metro at build time, so if these are ever exported before an EAS build the plaintext password ships in the JS/native bundle (readable via strings on the extracted binary). secrets.md sec.5 verified none of the three eas.json build profiles or any committed CI config sets them today — so it has never shipped — but nothing structurally prevents a future ad-hoc production build from doing so.
findings.recommendation: Gate the E2E auto-login seam so it is compiled out of production builds entirely (e.g. guard on __DEV__ or a non-EXPO_PUBLIC build flag, or strip the block in the production Metro config), and add an eas build preflight assertion that fails if EXPO_PUBLIC_E2E_* is present for a production profile. Track so it isn't silently reintroduced.

findings.title: Supabase leaked-password protection disabled while signup is client-direct — raises account-takeover risk
findings.detail: The Supabase security advisor flags auth_leaked_password_protection as OFF (advisors.md) — GoTrue is not checking new passwords against HaveIBeenPwned. Combined with client-direct, confirmations-off signup (SEC-01), users can set known-compromised passwords with no confirmation step, increasing credential-stuffing / account-takeover exposure for accounts holding health PII (weight, body measurements, sex-at-birth) and billing linkage. Low-cost one-toggle fix.
findings.recommendation: Enable leaked-password protection in the Supabase Auth dashboard; consider a minimum password policy at the same time. No code change required.

findings.title: /api/stripe/subscription-status makes an outbound Stripe API call per request with no rate limit
findings.detail: Of the authenticated non-cron routes without rateLimit(), subscription-status is the only one with a real per-call external cost: it hits Stripe's API server-side on every request (rate-limits.md sec.3). It is same-user scoped (.eq('id', userId)) so there is no cross-tenant abuse vector, but an authenticated user can spam their own status endpoint to burn Suppr's Stripe API quota with no cap. Low practical risk, flagged for quota hygiene.
findings.recommendation: Add a modest per-user rate limit (e.g. 10/min, matching /api/stripe/checkout) and/or a short server-side cache of the subscription-status response to avoid a live Stripe round-trip on every call.

findings.title: Broad anon/authenticated table grants remain on service-role-only billing tables (defense-in-depth gap)
findings.detail: revenuecat_events and stripe_webhook_events are safe today: RLS enabled + zero policies = deny-all for anon/authenticated (rls-status.md, advisors.md rls_enabled_no_policy). But grants.md shows both roles still carry the full SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE grant set on these billing-event tables. RLS is the only thing standing between authenticated users and raw billing/entitlement event data; if any future migration accidentally adds a permissive policy, the broad grants would immediately expose it. Not currently exploitable — a defense-in-depth hardening item.
findings.recommendation: REVOKE all grants from anon and authenticated on revenuecat_events and stripe_webhook_events (service_role bypasses RLS and keeps working). Same for promo_redeem_throttle and recipe_claims. This makes the deny-all posture belt-and-braces rather than RLS-only.

findings.title: SECURITY DEFINER RPCs are directly callable via PostgREST, bypassing Next.js route rate limits; household_join has no internal throttle
findings.detail: The advisors flag 10+ SECURITY DEFINER functions callable directly by anon/authenticated via /rest/v1/rpc/* (advisors.md). All sampled functions correctly re-derive identity from auth.uid() and set a fixed search_path (functions.md, auth-surface.md sec.6) — no RLS-bypass or privilege issue. But calling the RPC directly bypasses the rate limit on the sibling Next.js route: e.g. /api/household/join is capped at 5/min, yet household_join_by_invite_code is directly RPC-callable with no in-function throttle, relying solely on the ~48-bit invite-code entropy to resist brute force (infeasible, so low practical risk). The abuse-sensitive redemption RPCs (redeem_promo_code, redeem_referral_code) do carry internal throttles (promo 10/60s, referral velocity cap 5/24h), which is the right pattern.
findings.recommendation: Treat DB-layer throttling as the source of truth for abuse-sensitive RPCs, not the Next.js route wrapper. Add an internal attempt-throttle to household_join_by_invite_code (mirroring the promo_redeem_throttle pattern) so invite-code guessing is capped at the DB even when the route is bypassed. Audit any future SECURITY DEFINER RPC for whether its only rate limit lives in an app route that clients can skip.

findings.title: user_food_votes is fully readable by any authenticated user, exposing voter_id and vote across all users
findings.detail: The policy 'Authenticated users can read votes' on user_food_votes uses qual USING (true) for role authenticated (policies.md). Any authenticated user can read the entire table, correlating voter_id (an auth user id) to their up/down vote on each community food. Low-sensitivity behavioural data, but it is cross-user visibility of individual moderation activity that isn't needed client-side — only aggregate counts are (which are already materialized onto user_foods.upvotes/downvotes by a trigger).
findings.recommendation: Narrow the SELECT policy to own votes (voter_id = auth.uid()) and serve public tallies through the existing aggregate columns / count RPCs rather than exposing per-user vote rows. If the client genuinely needs to know 'did I vote', own-row scope already answers that.
evidenceRefs: policies.md user_food_votes 'Authenticated users can read votes'

findings.title: Hard-coded prod Supabase project makes any misconfigured env silently target live production instead of failing closed
findings.detail: utils/supabase/info.tsx (web) and apps/mobile/app.json (mobile) hard-code the live project ref fnfgxsignmuepshbebrl + anon key; publicConfig.ts falls back to them when NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_* is unset (secrets.md sec.4/5). The exposed material is only the anon key (public by design, RLS is the boundary) so this is not a credential leak — but the residual risk is operational: a fresh clone, cloud dev VM, or misconfigured preview silently reads/writes LIVE prod rather than erroring. With confirmations-off signup, a test signup lands real rows in prod. The team already documents the workaround (throwaway emails) rather than fixing it.
findings.recommendation: Prefer failing closed: require NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL/ANON_KEY in non-local environments and only fall back to the hard-coded pair when an explicit local/dev flag is set, so an unconfigured non-prod environment errors instead of silently hitting production. At minimum, keep the workaround documented and consider a separate non-prod project for dev/preview.

findings.title: /api/household POST invite-code rotation has no rate limit while sibling /api/household/join does
findings.detail: rate-limits.md sec.3 notes an inconsistency: /api/household/join (guessing someone else's code) is capped at 5/min, but /api/household POST { rotateInvite: true } (churning your own code) has no cap. The rotate update is scoped .eq('owner_id', userId), so the blast radius is self-DoS only (an owner invalidating their own outstanding invite) — no cross-tenant exposure. Minor consistency gap, not urgent.
findings.recommendation: Add a modest rate limit to the invite-rotation branch of /api/household POST for parity with /api/household/join. Low priority follow-up ticket.

findings.title: user_foods can be INSERTed already 'verified' — hostile user poisons the shared verified-food corpus every user reads
findings.detail: The UPDATE-side self-promotion hole on user_foods.verification_status is closed by the BEFORE UPDATE trigger user_foods_guard_status_transition (raises for non-admins on a status transition). But that trigger is BEFORE UPDATE ONLY (supabase/migrations/20260512100000_user_foods_p0_hardening.sql:154-156) and there is NO BEFORE INSERT guard on the table. The RLS INSERT policy 'Users can insert user foods' checks only WITH CHECK (submitted_by = auth.uid()) with no column restriction (policies.md, user_foods), 'authenticated' holds the INSERT grant (grants.md, user_foods), the column CHECK permits verification_status IN ('pending','verified','rejected') (20260414200001_user_foods_verification.sql:7-8), and upvotes/verified_at/verified_by are also unguarded on INSERT. So a hostile authenticated user, using the anon key + raw PostgREST (bypassing app/api/user-foods which hardcodes verification_status:'pending' at route.ts:181), can run: supabase.from('user_foods').insert({submitted_by:<self>, barcode:'<target>', name:'x', calories:<plausible-wrong>, verification_status:'verified', upvotes:999999}). This (1) passes grants+RLS+CHECK, (2) does not trip the UPDATE-only guard, (3) fires the AFTER INSERT trigger user_foods_after_status_change (functions.md) which, because tg_op='INSERT' and new.verification_status='verified', calls recompute_verified_food_canonical(barcode). recompute orders by upvotes DESC, so the attacker's row (upvotes 999999, verified_at now()) wins and is upserted into verified_food_canonical — the table lookupBarcode consults FIRST (20260512100000 comment) and every authenticated user can read (SELECT using(true)). The poisoned row is also directly visible in community food search (app/api/user-foods GET returns verification_status.eq.verified). Because the macro CHECK bounds (calories 0-2000, protein 0-100…) still pass, the attacker uses plausible-but-wrong values (a 500 kcal bar reported as 50 kcal), which evades sanity checks and silently corrupts every downstream user's calorie tracking with a 'verified' trust badge. This is the exact bug class ENG-1035 (20260611120000_profiles_insert_lockdown_eng1035.sql) fixed for profiles.user_tier with a dedicated BEFORE INSERT trigger — the same fix was never applied here.
findings.recommendation: Add a BEFORE INSERT trigger on public.user_foods that, for non-service_role (auth.role() <> 'service_role'), forces verification_status:='pending' and nulls verified_at/verified_by/upvotes/downvotes/flagged_for_admin_at — mirroring profiles_tier_column_insert_lockdown. Do NOT rely on the app route: the anon key + PostgREST bypasses it. Stage the migration and have Grace run supabase db push --linked (never MCP apply_migration). Add a test mirroring apps/mobile/tests/unit/userFoodsP0Hardening.test.ts asserting a direct born-verified insert is rejected/downgraded. Confidence 9/10.
evidenceRefs: evidence/live-db/policies.md#user_foods (INSERT with_check submitted_by=auth.uid(), no column restriction)
evidence/live-db/functions.md#user_foods_guard_status_transition (BEFORE UPDATE only), user_foods_after_status_change (AFTER INSERT promotes verified), recompute_verified_food_canonical
supabase/migrations/20260611120000_profiles_insert_lockdown_eng1035.sql (the born-privileged INSERT fix applied to profiles but not user_foods)

findings.title: barcode_mappings is directly client-writable — hostile user can mis-map any un-mapped barcode for all users
findings.detail: barcode_mappings has a public SELECT (using true) plus an INSERT policy barcode_mappings_write_own (WITH CHECK created_by = auth.uid()) and 'authenticated' holds the INSERT grant (policies.md/grants.md). barcode is the PRIMARY KEY (20260503101000_schema_drift_repair.sql:53-63) and is_verified/source have no write guard. So for any barcode without an existing mapping (the common case at launch), a hostile authenticated user can insert a mapping via PostgREST pointing food_id at any existing foods row of their choosing, with is_verified:true and source:'OpenFoodFacts' — misrepresenting provenance and redirecting every user's scan of that barcode to a wrong food. Blast radius is narrower than DI-01 (the PK prevents overwriting an already-mapped barcode, UPDATE is gated to created_by, and clients cannot insert into foods itself — foods has no INSERT policy, default-deny — so the attacker can only redirect to pre-existing food rows, not author arbitrary macros). Same root cause class as DI-01: RLS + grants permit direct client writes to a shared corpus with no server-controlled column guard. Read path: app/api/off/barcode consults barcode_mappings.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) drop the client INSERT/UPDATE policies on barcode_mappings and route all writes through app/api/barcode-mapping (service-role, which already tags created_by and can force is_verified=false), or (b) add a BEFORE INSERT/UPDATE trigger forcing is_verified:=false and source:='Community' for non-service_role writers. Verify the /api/off/barcode read prefers verified/service-created mappings. Confidence 8/10.

findings.title: recipe_collections migration merged but never applied to prod — shipped web+mobile Library code hits 'relation does not exist'
findings.detail: 20260703140000_eng1126_recipe_collections.sql (creates recipe_collections, recipe_collection_items, auth_owns_collection() + 7 RLS policies) was merged in commit afe735a9 / PR #754 on 2026-07-03 but was never pushed to the live DB: neither table exists in information_schema.tables, neither appears in policies.md/rls-status.md/grants.md, and supabase_migrations.schema_migrations has no row for version 20260703140000 (all confirmed read-only, rls-drift.md Finding #1). This is the exact 'db push --linked silently never run' hazard CLAUDE.md warns about. I confirmed the ENG-1126 feature code IS live in both shipped clients — src/context/appData/useRecipeCollectionsState.ts, src/app/components/Library.tsx, src/lib/recipes/recipeCollections.ts, apps/mobile/app/(tabs)/library.tsx, apps/mobile/lib/recipes.ts all reference these tables. So every user who opens the Recipes/Library surface and triggers a collections read/write currently gets a hard Postgres error in production. Not a security exposure — a live functional regression + a migration-discipline miss (drift in the opposite direction: files declare protection/structure that live lacks).
findings.recommendation: Stage 20260703140000_eng1126_recipe_collections.sql and have Grace run supabase db push --linked (never MCP apply_migration). Verify no newer migration depends on these tables (none found — it is the newest file). Confirm the ENG-1126 client paths degrade gracefully (not a white-screen) until the push lands. Add the merged-but-unapplied check to release-gate. Confidence 9/10.
evidenceRefs: evidence/rls-drift.md#Finding-1 (recipe_collections/recipe_collection_items never applied; schema_migrations has no 20260703140000)

findings.title: Confirmations-off signup + per-userId free-tier quotas = cheap account-farming to cycle LLM/vision spend during the viral ramp
findings.detail: Signup is client→GoTrue direct (supabase.auth.signUp in onboarding/steps/signup.tsx) with email confirmation OFF, returning a usable session synchronously; there is no Suppr-side proxy route, so rateLimit() never sees signup traffic and no app-level throttle exists upstream of GoTrue (rate-limits.md §4). Every expensive route (photo-log, voice-log, coach*, recipe-import image gen — Anthropic/OpenAI/Fal spend) is rate-limited per (userId, IP), and the free-tier AI quotas are keyed by userId — which means a fresh scripted account resets every quota. So an attacker who scripts account creation converts 'per-user cap' into 'per-account cap' and cycles paid LLM/vision calls at Suppr's cost. The only bound is GoTrue's built-in per-IP/per-email signup caps, which this repo neither configures, documents a value for, nor verifies; an IP-rotating attacker scales past them. Under the 2026-07-01 viral-push threat model this is the realistic 'unmetered cost' vector. Not a cross-user data exposure, so scored 4 not 5, but it should be hardened before the ramp widens.
findings.recommendation: Verify and tighten the GoTrue signup rate limits in the Supabase Dashboard (owner: Grace — config lives outside this repo) and record the chosen values in docs/security/auth.md so it is testable. Consider a lightweight signup proxy or hCaptcha/Turnstile on signup, and cap the highest-cost free-tier AI endpoints with an IP-scoped ceiling in addition to the userId bucket so a fresh account cannot fully reset spend. File a tracked ticket (no silent deferral). Confidence 7/10.

findings.title: EXPO_PUBLIC_E2E_PASSWORD would bake a plaintext auto-login password into any shipped bundle if ever set at build time
findings.detail: apps/mobile/context/auth.tsx has an E2E seam: with EXPO_PUBLIC_E2E_AUTH_ENABLED=true + EXPO_PUBLIC_E2E_EMAIL/PASSWORD set, the app auto-signs-in in plaintext. Because these are EXPO_PUBLIC_* (Metro inlines them at build time), any EAS build with them set ships the plaintext password in the JS bundle (readable via strings). Confirmed NOT currently exercised — none of the three eas.json profiles set them, CI uses unprefixed E2E_EMAIL/PASSWORD for web Playwright only, and no committed config sets EXPO_PUBLIC_E2E_AUTH_ENABLED (secrets.md §5). Latent process risk, not an active leak.
findings.recommendation: Rename the seam to non-EXPO_PUBLIC vars read at runtime, or gate it behind __DEV__ so it can never be inlined into a release bundle; add an eas build guard/CI assertion that fails if any EXPO_PUBLIC_E2E_* is present in a production profile. Track as a follow-up. Confidence 8/10.

findings.title: anon role has no SELECT grant on public.recipes — logged-out PostgREST reads of shared recipe links would fail
findings.detail: grants.md shows anon lacks the table-level SELECT grant on public.recipes (authenticated has it); the RLS policy recipes_select_published_own_or_saved would otherwise permit reading published recipes. Postgres/PostgREST still require the table SELECT grant for an RLS-gated read to return rows, so any logged-out client reading a shared recipe directly via PostgREST gets nothing. rls-drift.md flags this was set at initial (pre-migration) table creation, not by any tracked migration. For the viral share hook (a Reel-imported recipe link opened by a logged-out visitor) this could break the landing read — UNLESS those pages are server-rendered with an authenticated/service context. This is a verification item, not a confirmed break: I did not trace whether the public recipe share path uses anon PostgREST or SSR.
findings.recommendation: Trace the logged-out public recipe read path. If it uses the anon PostgREST client, add GRANT SELECT ON public.recipes TO anon in a tracked migration (RLS still restricts to published rows) and pin it. If it is SSR-only with a server client, document the intentional anon-revoke so a future reviewer does not 'fix' it. Confidence 6/10.

findings.title: Hard-coded prod Supabase project (anon key) means unconfigured environments silently target live production
findings.detail: utils/supabase/info.tsx (web) and apps/mobile/app.json (mobile) hard-code the prod project ref fnfgxsignmuepshbebrl + the anon key as a fallback when NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_* / expo.extra are unset (secrets.md §4-5). The exposed material is anon-only (public by design; RLS is the boundary), so this is not a credential leak — the residual risk is operational: a fresh clone, cloud dev VM, or misconfigured preview writes to LIVE prod instead of failing closed. Already documented for the cloud-agent case in apps/mobile/CLAUDE.md ('test signups/writes hit the real prod DB; use throwaway emails') — a known workaround, not a fix. Compounds DI-01/DI-02 (test poisoning writes would land in prod). Recorded for completeness, not re-litigated as a leak.
findings.recommendation: Make the prod fallback opt-in: in non-production builds, fail closed (throw a clear 'set NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_* / expo.extra' error) rather than silently defaulting to prod, or resolve to a dev project. At minimum keep the documented guardrail current. Confidence 7/10.

findings.title: Unused edge-function stub ships CORS origin:'*' — not deployed, cleanup only
findings.detail: supabase/functions/server/index.tsx is a 25-line Hono stub with a single unauthenticated /make-server-58b1cc02/health route and CORS wildcarded to origin:'*'. Confirmed via live list_edge_functions that ZERO edge functions are deployed on the project, and nothing in src/app/apps references it (auth-surface.md §5). No current exposure (not deployed, no data access) — purely dead scaffold worth removing so it cannot be deployed later by accident.
findings.recommendation: Delete supabase/functions/server/ (or, if kept as a template, tighten the CORS origin and note it is a scaffold). Low priority. Confidence 9/10.
